paragraphs.body: Should I expect to see squirrels at Walnut Street? Among the places I had heard of in Pittsburgh before I ever came to this city, Walnut Street might be the most modest one. It is neither expansive – as we can see on the map – nor famous, but it does have an intriguing name. Why is it called Walnut Street? Does it have anything to do with the well-known Squirrel Hill? Should I expect to see squirrels eating walnuts there?

The first time I went to Walnut Street was when a friend of mine told me about a well-rated thrift store there. She said, “We should shop for some styles,” and I totally agreed, even though I had never tried thrifting before. Thrifting simply was not a part of the culture where I grew up, but why not try something new – just like Walnut Street? Image 1 Prompt: A city street with stores, restaurants, walnuts, and squirrels Would it be a street full of walnuts and squirrels? Would it have a European style, given Pittsburgh’s European-style architecture? Or would it be petite, unique, and decorated? With these fore-feelings, my first visit to Walnut Street was a blend of perfections and imperfections. The thrift store was not particularly stylish, which was understandable since Pittsburgh is certainly not a city of fashion. However, we found a Peruvian store selling handmade traditional goods, and that exact store made the trip worthwhile. We met the store owner; her reading glasses perched on the middle of her nose bridge and her Peruvian Spanish accent instantly reminded me of scenes from Cien años de Soledad. We spotted a Vietnamese restaurant where we later had pho in. We noticed various Chinese, Mexican and Italian dinners. These diverse offerings perfectly summed up Walnut Street – I had expected squirrels eating walnuts, but instead, I found a cultural intersection. Image 2 Prompt: A city street with stores and restaurants of different cultures, walnuts, and squirrels Walnut street certainly reminded me of a street in my home country called Beijing Road. I am from Guangzhou, and we have a street named after the city of Beijing. Isn’t that a testament to how entwined cultures are? Just like Walnut Street, Beijing Road is deeply rooted in tradition and culture. It is built upon early 20th century architectures, and it is a place to savor various traditional Chinese cuisines from across the country. Beijing road and Walnut Street share the very same core characteristic behind but are with different expressions: for instance, Walnut Street is a hub of stores and restaurants from across the world, while Beijing Road offers cuisines from different regions of China, and focuses more on those detailed differences between different types of Chinese food. Image 3 Prompt: a southern Chinese pedestrian road with early 20th century architecture In hindsight, expecting to see squirrels eating walnuts on Walnut Street wasn’t entirely wrong. Walnut Street does owe its name to the many walnut trees throughout the street, but sadly I did not come across any squirrels on my first visit. However, DALL.E made some stereotypical assumptions about Walnut Street and Beijing Road. Based on the description of Walnut Street on Wikipedia, DALL.E did not expect Walnut Street to be a place with stores and restaurant of different cultures, and thought Beijing Road was a road in Beijing. But is it entirely DALL.E’s fault? I don’t think so. 当我们刻意以“是否有刻板印象”的眼光审视generative AI 的产 物，以达成某种用以消除语言或文化刻板印象的结论，我们是否已经过于苛刻？我们所提 供的纯文字的prompt 之于DALL.E，也许只相当于纯文字的地名——不管是“Walnut Street”还是“北京路”——之于我们；而要求仅仅从这类文字信息中公正地构建一个地 点的形象，也许本身就是有失偏颇的。我想，与其基于此反思自身对语言和文化的理解的 局限，不如得出一条更有意义的结论：亲身体验是完整了解一个地点的最好方法。 Image 4: Comparison between DALL.E generated images of Walnut Street and Beijing Road Prompt: A street named Walnut Street that is best known for its upscale shopping, fine dining, and private boutiques; a southern Chinese pedestrian road with early 20th century architecture named Beijing Road As far as I can tell, DALL.E did its best in generating neutral images. The two images above did not look exactly like Walnut Street and Beijing Road – Walnut Street appeared messier in real life than in the image, and Beijing Road was less splendid – but they captured the essence of what these places are about. In other words, learning about a place from AI-generated content may not lead to major cultural misunderstandings, but it still requires a closer look to acquire authentic information.
